tbs.title: Segment Reporting - Quantitative Thresholds
tbs.scenario:
Consolidated Industries has identified five operating segments. Management needs to determine which segments require separate disclosure under ASC 280.

Segment Data (in thousands):
                    Revenue    Profit/Loss    Assets
Segment A           $45,000      $8,000      $35,000
Segment B           $30,000      $4,500      $25,000
Segment C           $15,000     ($2,000)     $20,000
Segment D            $8,000      $1,200      $15,000
Segment E            $2,000        $300       $5,000

Totals:            $100,000     $12,000     $100,000

For profit/loss test, use absolute values:
• Total profits: $14,000
• Total losses: $2,000
• Greater absolute value: $14,000

Apply ASC 280 quantitative thresholds.

requirements.question: Which segments meet the 10% REVENUE threshold?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

10% of $100,000 = $10,000. Segments with revenue ≥ $10,000: A ($45,000), B ($30,000), C ($15,000). D and E do not meet the revenue threshold.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Which segments meet the 10% PROFIT/LOSS threshold (using the greater of absolute profits or losses)?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

10% of $14,000 (greater absolute value) = $1,400. Segments meeting threshold: A ($8,000), B ($4,500), C ($2,000 loss). D ($1,200) does not meet threshold.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Which segments meet the 10% ASSETS threshold?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

10% of $100,000 = $10,000. Segments with assets ≥ $10,000: A ($35,000), B ($25,000), C ($20,000), D ($15,000). Only E ($5,000) does not meet the threshold.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Which segments are reportable (meet ANY threshold)?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

A segment is reportable if it meets ANY of the three thresholds. A (all 3), B (all 3), C (all 3), D (assets only) are reportable. E meets no thresholds.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: What percentage of total revenue is represented by reportable segments A, B, C, and D?
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Reportable segment revenue: $45,000 + $30,000 + $15,000 + $8,000 = $98,000. Percentage: $98,000 / $100,000 = 98%. This exceeds the 75% threshold, so no additional segments need to be reported.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Test each threshold separately: revenue, profit/loss, assets
For profit/loss, use greater of absolute profits or absolute losses
A segment meeting ANY threshold is reportable
Reportable segments must represent ≥ 75% of total revenue

tbs.title: Business Combination - Acquisition Method
tbs.scenario:
Alpha Corp. acquires 100% of Beta Inc. on January 1, Year 1. The following information is available at the acquisition date:

• Cash paid by Alpha: $2,500,000
• Fair value of Alpha stock issued (100,000 shares at $15/share): $1,500,000
• Contingent consideration (fair value at acquisition date): $200,000
• Direct acquisition costs paid by Alpha: $75,000

Beta Inc.'s balance sheet at acquisition date (book values):
• Cash: $300,000
• Accounts Receivable: $450,000
• Inventory: $600,000
• Equipment (net): $1,200,000
• Total Assets: $2,550,000
• Accounts Payable: $350,000
• Long-term Debt: $800,000
• Common Stock: $500,000
• Retained Earnings: $900,000
• Total Liabilities & Equity: $2,550,000

Fair value adjustments:
• Inventory fair value: $650,000
• Equipment fair value: $1,400,000
• Customer relationships (identifiable intangible, not previously recorded): $300,000
• In-process R&D (identifiable intangible): $150,000

requirements.question: Calculate the total consideration transferred in this business combination.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Total consideration = Cash ($2,500,000) + Stock ($1,500,000) + Contingent consideration ($200,000) = $4,200,000. Direct acquisition costs are expensed, not included in consideration.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the fair value of identifiable net assets acquired.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Fair value of net assets = Cash ($300,000) + AR ($450,000) + Inventory ($650,000) + Equipment ($1,400,000) + Customer relationships ($300,000) + R&D ($150,000) - AP ($350,000) - Debt ($800,000) = $2,900,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the goodwill recognized in this acquisition.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Goodwill = Consideration ($4,200,000) - Fair value of net assets ($2,900,000) = $1,300,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Prepare the journal entry to record the acquisition on Alpha's books.
Prepare the journal entry to record the direct acquisition costs.

hints: Under ASC 805, direct acquisition costs are EXPENSED, not capitalized
Include ALL forms of consideration: cash, stock, and contingent consideration
Identifiable intangibles (customer relationships, R&D) are recognized at fair value even if not on acquiree's books

tbs.title: Consolidated Financial Statements - Intercompany Eliminations
tbs.scenario:
Parent Corp. owns 80% of Sub Inc. (acquired at book value, no goodwill). The following intercompany transactions occurred during Year 1:

1. Parent sold inventory to Sub for $500,000. Parent's cost was $350,000. Sub still holds 40% of this inventory at year-end.

2. Sub sold land to Parent for $300,000. Sub's cost was $220,000.

3. Parent made management fee charges to Sub of $120,000 during the year.

4. Sub declared dividends of $100,000 during the year.

5. At year-end, Sub owes Parent $85,000 for inventory purchases.

Year-end trial balances (before consolidation adjustments):

Parent Corp:
• Sales: $4,000,000
• Cost of Sales: $2,400,000
• Management Fee Income: $120,000
• Dividend Income: $80,000
• Investment in Sub: $800,000

Sub Inc:
• Sales: $2,500,000
• Cost of Sales: $1,600,000
• Management Fee Expense: $120,000
• Gain on Sale of Land: $80,000
• Dividends Declared: $100,000

requirements.question: Calculate the unrealized profit in ending inventory that must be eliminated.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Unrealized profit = ($500,000 - $350,000) × 40% = $150,000 × 40% = $60,000. This represents the markup still in Sub's inventory.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the intercompany sales to be eliminated.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

All intercompany sales ($500,000) must be eliminated against the corresponding cost of sales.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the gain on land sale to be eliminated.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

The entire gain ($300,000 - $220,000 = $80,000) on the intercompany land sale must be eliminated.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the noncontrolling interest share of Sub's adjusted net income.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Sub's adjusted net income = Sales ($2,500,000) - Cost of Sales ($1,600,000) - Mgmt Fee ($120,000) - Unrealized land gain ($80,000) = $700,000 - $80,000 = $620,000. NCI share = $620,000 × 20% + adjustment for downstream profit = varies based on transaction direction. If downstream (Parent to Sub), the unrealized inventory profit stays with Parent.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Which intercompany elimination affects consolidated Cost of Goods Sold?
options: Inventory sale and unrealized profit eliminations
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

The inventory sale elimination reduces both Sales and COGS. The unrealized profit in ending inventory increases consolidated COGS (reduces gross profit).

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Intercompany sales eliminate 100% regardless of ownership percentage
For downstream sales (parent to sub), unrealized profit is attributed 100% to parent
For upstream sales (sub to parent), unrealized profit is allocated between parent and NCI

tbs.title: Noncontrolling Interest Calculation
tbs.scenario:
On January 1, Year 1, Parent Corp. acquired 75% of Subsidiary Inc. for $1,500,000 when Subsidiary's identifiable net assets had a fair value of $1,800,000.

Information at acquisition:
• Book value of Subsidiary's net assets: $1,600,000
• Fair value of Subsidiary's net assets: $1,800,000
• Fair value of NCI at acquisition: $500,000

Year 1 activity for Subsidiary:
• Net income: $400,000
• Dividends declared: $100,000
• Other comprehensive income (unrealized gains): $50,000

No impairment of goodwill occurred during Year 1.

requirements.question: Calculate the goodwill at the acquisition date using the full goodwill method.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Full goodwill = (Consideration paid + Fair value of NCI) - Fair value of net assets = ($1,500,000 + $500,000) - $1,800,000 = $200,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the NCI share of net income for Year 1.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

NCI share of net income = $400,000 × 25% = $100,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the NCI share of dividends for Year 1.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

NCI share of dividends = $100,000 × 25% = $25,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the ending NCI balance in equity at December 31, Year 1.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Ending NCI = Beginning NCI ($500,000) + NCI share of net income ($100,000) - NCI share of dividends ($25,000) + NCI share of OCI ($50,000 × 25% = $12,500) = $587,500

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Under full goodwill method, NCI is initially recorded at fair value
NCI balance changes by its share of subsidiary's comprehensive income and dividends
Remember to include OCI items in the NCI calculation

tbs.title: Derivatives and Fair Value Hedge Accounting
tbs.scenario:
On October 1, Year 1, Delta Corp. holds inventory with a carrying value of $2,000,000 and a fair value of $2,000,000. To hedge against a potential decline in value, Delta enters into a forward contract to sell the inventory on March 31, Year 2.

Forward contract details:
• Notional amount: $2,000,000
• Forward price: $2,000,000
• Delta designates this as a fair value hedge of inventory
• The hedge is highly effective

Fair value changes:
• December 31, Year 1:
  - Inventory fair value: $1,920,000
  - Forward contract fair value: $75,000 asset

• March 31, Year 2:
  - Inventory fair value: $1,850,000
  - Forward contract fair value: $150,000 asset

requirements.question: Calculate the loss on hedged inventory to be recognized at December 31, Year 1.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Loss on inventory = $2,000,000 - $1,920,000 = $80,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the gain on the forward contract at December 31, Year 1.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Gain on forward = $75,000 - $0 = $75,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the net income effect from the hedge at December 31, Year 1.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Net income effect = Gain on forward ($75,000) - Loss on inventory ($80,000) = -$5,000 (net loss)

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Prepare the journal entry to record the forward contract gain and inventory adjustment at December 31, Year 1.
In a fair value hedge, where are gains and losses on the hedging instrument recognized?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

In a fair value hedge, gains/losses on both the hedging instrument AND the hedged item are recognized in current earnings.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Fair value hedge: Both hedging instrument and hedged item gains/losses go to earnings
Cash flow hedge: Hedging instrument gains/losses go to OCI
The goal is to offset volatility in earnings from the hedged item

tbs.title: Foreign Currency Translation vs Remeasurement
tbs.scenario:
US Parent has a wholly-owned subsidiary, Euro Sub, located in Germany. Euro Sub's functional currency is the Euro (€).

Euro Sub's December 31, Year 1 trial balance (in Euros):
• Cash: €500,000
• Accounts Receivable: €800,000
• Inventory (at cost): €600,000
• Equipment (net): €2,000,000
• Accounts Payable: €400,000
• Long-term Debt: €1,200,000
• Common Stock: €1,000,000
• Retained Earnings (1/1): €700,000
• Sales Revenue: €3,500,000
• Cost of Goods Sold: €2,100,000
• Depreciation Expense: €200,000
• Other Expenses: €600,000

Exchange rates:
• January 1, Year 1 (historical rate when equipment acquired): $1.10/€
• Average rate for Year 1: $1.15/€
• December 31, Year 1: $1.20/€
• Rate when common stock issued: $1.05/€
• Rate when beginning RE was earned (weighted average historical): $1.08/€

requirements.question: Using the current rate method (translation), calculate translated Cash in USD.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Cash is a monetary asset, translated at current rate: €500,000 × $1.20 = $600,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Using the current rate method, calculate translated Equipment (net) in USD.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Under current rate method (translation), ALL assets use current rate: €2,000,000 × $1.20 = $2,400,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Using the current rate method, calculate translated Sales Revenue in USD.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Revenue uses average rate: €3,500,000 × $1.15 = $4,025,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate Euro Sub's net income in Euros.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Net income = Revenue (€3,500,000) - COGS (€2,100,000) - Depreciation (€200,000) - Other (€600,000) = €600,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Under the current rate method (translation), where is the translation adjustment reported?
options: In net income as a foreign exchange gain/loss
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Translation adjustments under the current rate method are reported in OCI and accumulated in the cumulative translation adjustment (CTA) in equity.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Translation (current rate method): Assets/Liabilities at current rate, Revenues/Expenses at average rate, Equity at historical rates
Remeasurement (temporal method): Monetary items at current, Non-monetary at historical
Translation adjustment goes to OCI; Remeasurement gain/loss goes to income

tbs.title: Governmental Capital Assets and Infrastructure
tbs.scenario:
City of Springfield engages in the following capital asset transactions during the fiscal year:

1. Purchased a new fire truck for $450,000 cash from the General Fund.

2. Constructed a new road segment using General Fund resources:
   • Direct construction costs: $2,800,000
   • Interest during construction: $150,000 (capitalized under modified approach)
   • Engineering and design: $250,000

3. Received a donated park from a local developer:
   • Estimated fair value of land: $800,000
   • Estimated fair value of improvements: $200,000

4. The city uses the modified approach for infrastructure assets. The road network meets the condition assessment requirements.

5. Depreciation for the year on governmental capital assets (excluding infrastructure under modified approach): $385,000

The city has separate governmental funds and government-wide financial statements.

requirements.question: Prepare the General Fund journal entry to record the fire truck purchase.
Prepare the government-wide journal entry to record the fire truck purchase.
Calculate the total cost of the constructed road to be capitalized at the government-wide level.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Total road cost = Construction ($2,800,000) + Interest ($150,000) + Engineering ($250,000) = $3,200,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Prepare the government-wide journal entry to record the donated park.
Under the modified approach for infrastructure, which statement is correct?
options: Depreciation is required on all infrastructure
Infrastructure must be depreciated but can use any method
Depreciation is NOT required if the government commits to maintain condition levels
The modified approach only applies to donated infrastructure
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Under the modified approach (GASB 34), governments can elect NOT to depreciate infrastructure if they: (1) manage using an asset management system, (2) perform condition assessments, and (3) demonstrate that infrastructure is maintained at or above the established condition level.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: General Fund uses modified accrual - capital expenditures recorded as expenditures
Government-wide uses full accrual - capital assets are capitalized
Donated assets are recorded at acquisition value (fair value)

tbs.title: Pension Accounting (GASB 68)
tbs.scenario:
The City of Riverside participates in a cost-sharing multiple-employer defined benefit pension plan. The following information is available for fiscal year ending June 30, Year 1:

Plan-wide information:
• Total pension liability (all employers): $500,000,000
• Plan fiduciary net position: $420,000,000
• Net pension liability (all employers): $80,000,000
• City's proportionate share: 2.5%

City of Riverside's pension information:
• Beginning net pension liability (city's share): $1,800,000
• Pension expense for the year: $650,000
• Contributions to the plan: $575,000
• City's ending net pension liability: (to be calculated)

Deferred outflows/inflows related to pensions:
• Beginning deferred outflows: $250,000
• Beginning deferred inflows: $180,000
• Changes in assumptions (deferred outflow): $85,000
• Difference between expected/actual experience (deferred inflow): $45,000

requirements.question: Calculate the City's ending net pension liability (its proportionate share).
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

City's proportionate share of NPL = $80,000,000 × 2.5% = $2,000,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the change in net pension liability for the year.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Change in NPL = Ending ($2,000,000) - Beginning ($1,800,000) = $200,000 increase

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the ending deferred outflows of resources related to pensions.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Ending deferred outflows = Beginning ($250,000) + Changes in assumptions ($85,000) = $335,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the ending deferred inflows of resources related to pensions.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Ending deferred inflows = Beginning ($180,000) + Experience difference ($45,000) = $225,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: In governmental financial statements, pension expense is recognized:
options: Only in the proprietary fund statements
In both governmental fund and government-wide statements
In government-wide statements and proprietary funds, but not governmental funds
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Pension expense is recognized in government-wide statements (full accrual) and proprietary fund statements. Governmental funds use modified accrual and recognize pension expenditures based on amounts due and payable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Net pension liability = Total pension liability - Plan fiduciary net position
Proportionate share is based on employer contributions relative to total contributions
Deferred outflows/inflows are amortized to pension expense over time

tbs.title: Comprehensive Financial Ratio Analysis
tbs.scenario:
Apex Industries presents the following financial data for Year 1:

Balance Sheet (December 31, Year 1):
• Cash: $150,000
• Accounts Receivable (net): $400,000
• Inventory: $500,000
• Prepaid Expenses: $50,000
• Property, Plant & Equipment (net): $2,000,000
• Total Assets: $3,100,000

• Accounts Payable: $300,000
• Accrued Liabilities: $100,000
• Current Portion of Long-term Debt: $100,000
• Long-term Debt: $800,000
• Common Stock: $1,000,000
• Retained Earnings: $800,000
• Total Liabilities & Equity: $3,100,000

Income Statement (Year 1):
• Net Sales: $4,500,000
• Cost of Goods Sold: $2,700,000
• Gross Profit: $1,800,000
• Operating Expenses: $1,200,000
• Interest Expense: $72,000
• Income Tax Expense: $158,400
• Net Income: $369,600

Additional Information:
• Average accounts receivable: $380,000
• Average inventory: $450,000
• Average total assets: $2,900,000

requirements.question: Calculate the current ratio.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Current ratio = Current assets / Current liabilities = ($150,000 + $400,000 + $500,000 + $50,000) / ($300,000 + $100,000 + $100,000) = $1,100,000 / $500,000 = 2.2

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the quick ratio (acid-test ratio).
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Quick ratio = (Cash + Receivables) / Current liabilities = ($150,000 + $400,000) / $500,000 = 1.1

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate accounts receivable turnover.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

AR turnover = Net sales / Average AR = $4,500,000 / $380,000 = 11.84 times

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate inventory turnover.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Inventory turnover = COGS / Average inventory = $2,700,000 / $450,000 = 6.0 times

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate return on assets (ROA).
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

ROA = Net income / Average total assets = $369,600 / $2,900,000 = 12.74%

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the debt-to-equity ratio.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Debt-to-equity = Total liabilities / Total equity = ($300,000 + $100,000 + $100,000 + $800,000) / ($1,000,000 + $800,000) = $1,300,000 / $1,800,000 = 0.72

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Current assets include cash, receivables, inventory, and prepaids
Quick ratio excludes inventory and prepaids
Use average balances for turnover and ROA calculations when available

tbs.title: Cost-Volume-Profit Analysis
tbs.scenario:
Precision Manufacturing produces a single product with the following cost structure:

• Selling price per unit: $85
• Variable manufacturing cost per unit: $35
• Variable selling expense per unit: $10
• Fixed manufacturing overhead: $300,000 per year
• Fixed selling and administrative: $150,000 per year

The company's current sales volume is 15,000 units per year.

Management is considering several alternatives:
1. Increase advertising by $50,000 to boost sales by 3,000 units
2. Reduce selling price by $5 per unit, expecting a 25% increase in unit sales
3. Outsource production, eliminating variable manufacturing and fixed manufacturing overhead, but increasing variable cost to $50 per unit

requirements.question: Calculate the contribution margin per unit.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Contribution margin = Selling price - Variable costs = $85 - $35 - $10 = $40 per unit

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the break-even point in units.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Break-even units = Total fixed costs / CM per unit = ($300,000 + $150,000) / $40 = 11,250 units

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate current operating income.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Operating income = (Units × CM per unit) - Fixed costs = (15,000 × $40) - $450,000 = $600,000 - $450,000 = $150,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate operating income under Alternative 1 (additional advertising).
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Alternative 1: New units = 18,000. Operating income = (18,000 × $40) - $450,000 - $50,000 = $720,000 - $500,000 = $220,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate operating income under Alternative 2 (price reduction).
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Alternative 2: New price = $80, New CM = $35. New units = 15,000 × 1.25 = 18,750. Operating income = (18,750 × $35) - $450,000 = $656,250 - $450,000 = $206,250

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Which alternative provides the highest operating income?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Alternative 1 (advertising) provides the highest operating income at $220,000.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Contribution margin = Revenue - ALL variable costs
Break-even: Total contribution = Total fixed costs
Consider incremental analysis for decision-making

tbs.title: Variance Analysis - Manufacturing
tbs.scenario:
Standard Manufacturing uses a standard cost system. The following standards were established for direct materials and direct labor:

Direct Materials:
• Standard quantity: 3 pounds per unit
• Standard price: $8 per pound

Direct Labor:
• Standard hours: 2 hours per unit
• Standard rate: $22 per hour

Actual results for March (5,000 units produced):
Direct Materials:
• Pounds purchased and used: 16,000 pounds
• Actual price paid: $7.50 per pound
• Actual total cost: $120,000

Direct Labor:
• Actual hours worked: 10,500 hours
• Actual rate paid: $23 per hour
• Actual total cost: $241,500

Variable Manufacturing Overhead:
• Standard rate: $6 per direct labor hour
• Actual variable overhead: $64,000

requirements.question: Calculate the direct materials price variance.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Price variance = (SP - AP) × AQ = ($8 - $7.50) × 16,000 = $0.50 × 16,000 = $8,000 Favorable

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the direct materials quantity variance.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Quantity variance = (SQ - AQ) × SP = (15,000 - 16,000) × $8 = -1,000 × $8 = $8,000 Unfavorable (use absolute value)

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the direct labor rate variance.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Rate variance = (SR - AR) × AH = ($22 - $23) × 10,500 = -$1 × 10,500 = $10,500 Unfavorable

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the direct labor efficiency variance.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Efficiency variance = (SH - AH) × SR = (10,000 - 10,500) × $22 = -500 × $22 = $11,000 Unfavorable

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the variable overhead efficiency variance.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

VOH efficiency variance = (SH - AH) × Standard VOH rate = (10,000 - 10,500) × $6 = -500 × $6 = $3,000 Unfavorable

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: The materials price variance is favorable. What is the most likely cause?
options: Workers used more material than standard
Purchasing obtained a better price than expected
More units were produced than planned
The standard price was set too low
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

A favorable price variance means actual price < standard price, likely due to better purchasing negotiations, bulk discounts, or market conditions.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Standard quantity for actual production = Standard per unit × Units produced
Standard hours for actual production = Standard per unit × Units produced
Favorable variance: Actual < Standard cost

tbs.title: Variance Analysis - Manufacturing
tbs.scenario:
Phoenix Manufacturing produces industrial valves. Standard cost data for one unit:

Direct Materials:
• Standard quantity: 4 pounds per unit
• Standard price: $8.00 per pound

Direct Labor:
• Standard hours: 2.5 hours per unit
• Standard rate: $22.00 per hour

Variable Overhead:
• Applied based on direct labor hours
• Standard rate: $6.00 per DLH

Actual results for March (10,000 units produced):
• Materials purchased: 42,000 pounds at $7.80/pound
• Materials used: 41,500 pounds
• Direct labor: 24,200 hours at $22.50/hour
• Variable overhead incurred: $150,000

requirements.question: Calculate the direct materials price variance.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

(AP - SP) × AQ purchased = ($7.80 - $8.00) × 42,000 = -$8,400 Favorable

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the direct materials quantity variance.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

(AQ used - SQ allowed) × SP = (41,500 - 40,000) × $8.00 = $12,000 Unfavorable

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the direct labor rate variance.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

(AR - SR) × AH = ($22.50 - $22.00) × 24,200 = $12,100 Unfavorable

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the direct labor efficiency variance.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

(AH - SH allowed) × SR = (24,200 - 25,000) × $22.00 = -$17,600 Favorable. SH = 10,000 × 2.5 = 25,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the variable overhead efficiency variance.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

(AH - SH) × Standard VOH rate = (24,200 - 25,000) × $6.00 = -$4,800 Favorable

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Price variance calculated at purchase; quantity at use
SQ allowed = Actual units × Standard quantity per unit
Efficiency variances use standard rates
Favorable = Actual < Standard cost

tbs.title: Capital Budgeting - NPV and IRR
tbs.scenario:
Apex Corp. is evaluating a new production line:

Initial Investment: $2,000,000
Useful life: 5 years
Salvage value: $200,000
Tax rate: 25%
WACC: 10%

Annual Operating Data:
• Revenue increase: $1,200,000
• Cash operating costs: $600,000
• Depreciation: Straight-line to salvage

Present Value Factors at 10%:
| Year | PV of $1 | PV of Annuity |
|------|----------|---------------|
| 1 | 0.909 | 0.909 |
| 2 | 0.826 | 1.736 |
| 3 | 0.751 | 2.487 |
| 4 | 0.683 | 3.170 |
| 5 | 0.621 | 3.791 |

requirements.question: Calculate annual depreciation expense.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

($2,000,000 - $200,000) / 5 years = $360,000 per year

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate annual after-tax operating cash flow.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Before tax: $1,200,000 - $600,000 = $600,000. Tax: ($600,000 - $360,000) × 25% = $60,000. After-tax CF = $600,000 - $60,000 = $540,000. Or: EBIT × (1-T) + Dep = $240,000 × 0.75 + $360,000 = $540,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the NPV of the project.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

PV of operating CFs: $540,000 × 3.791 = $2,047,140. PV of salvage: $200,000 × 0.621 = $124,200. Total PV = $2,171,340. NPV = $2,171,340 - $2,000,000 = $171,340

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Should Apex accept this project based on NPV?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

NPV > 0 indicates the project adds value. Accept projects with positive NPV.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Is the IRR above or below 10%?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Since NPV is positive at 10%, the IRR must be higher than 10% (the discount rate where NPV = 0).

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Depreciation tax shield = Depreciation × Tax rate
After-tax CF = (Revenue - Costs)(1-T) + Depreciation × T
NPV positive → Accept; IRR > WACC
Salvage value may have tax implications if ≠ book value

tbs.title: Segment Reporting Analysis

requirements.question: Calculate the revenue threshold for reportable segments.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

10% of total revenue = $30,000,000 × 10% = $3,000,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the profit/loss threshold (use absolute values).
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Greater of: (1) 10% of combined profit = $3,600,000 × 10% = $360,000, or (2) 10% of combined loss = $500,000 × 10% = $50,000. Use $360,000.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the asset threshold for reportable segments.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

10% of total assets = $40,000,000 × 10% = $4,000,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Which segments are separately reportable?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Alpha: Meets all 3 thresholds. Beta: Meets revenue ($8M), loss ($500K > $360K), assets ($12M). Gamma: Meets revenue ($4M), profit ($800K). Delta: Meets revenue ($3M) and assets ($4M). All 4 are reportable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: What is required for segment reconciliation?
options: Reconcile segment totals to consolidated amounts only
Reconcile revenues, profit/loss, and assets to consolidated
Reconcile only if segments exceed 75% test
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

ASC 280 requires reconciliation of segment revenues, profit/loss, and assets to consolidated financial statement amounts.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Reportable if ≥ 10% of revenue, profit/loss, OR assets
Profit test: Greater of 10% combined profits or 10% combined losses
75% test: Reportable segments must be ≥ 75% of external revenue
Reconciliation required for all key measures

tbs.title: Ratio Analysis and Trend Interpretation
tbs.scenario:
Analyze the following 3-year trend for Retail Solutions Inc.:

| Ratio | Year 1 | Year 2 | Year 3 | Industry Avg |
|-------|--------|--------|--------|--------------|
| Current Ratio | 2.1 | 1.8 | 1.4 | 1.6 |
| Quick Ratio | 1.2 | 0.9 | 0.6 | 0.9 |
| Inventory Turnover | 8.0 | 6.5 | 5.2 | 7.0 |
| Days Sales Outstanding | 35 | 42 | 55 | 40 |
| Debt-to-Equity | 0.8 | 1.2 | 1.6 | 1.0 |
| Interest Coverage | 6.0 | 4.2 | 2.8 | 4.5 |

Additional Information:
• Year 3 sales increased 15% over Year 2
• Gross margin remained stable at 35%
• The company opened 5 new stores in Year 3

requirements.question: What is the primary liquidity concern?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Quick ratio declining faster than current ratio indicates inventory buildup. Rising DSO (35→55 days) shows slowing collections. Both point to liquidity stress from working capital issues.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: What do the solvency ratios indicate?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Debt-to-equity doubled (0.8→1.6), now above industry (1.0). Interest coverage halved (6.0→2.8), below industry (4.5). Both indicate increasing financial risk and potential solvency issues.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: What likely caused the ratio deterioration?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Sales increased 15%, margins stable, but 5 new stores opened. Rising debt and declining coverage suggest expansion was debt-funded. Inventory turnover decline suggests new stores may be overstocked.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Which ratio is most concerning for short-term creditors?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Quick ratio at 0.6 (vs 0.9 industry) indicates inability to meet short-term obligations without selling inventory. For creditors needing quick repayment, this is most concerning.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Quick ratio excludes inventory from current assets
DSO increase = slower collection of receivables
Interest coverage below 3.0 is generally concerning
Rapid expansion often strains working capital

tbs.title: Transfer Pricing Analysis
tbs.scenario:
Omega Corp. has two divisions:

**Components Division** (seller):
• Variable cost per unit: $45
• Fixed costs: $500,000 (allocated based on capacity)
• Capacity: 100,000 units
• Current external sales: 70,000 units at $80/unit
• No additional fixed costs for internal sales

**Assembly Division** (buyer):
• Needs 25,000 units of the component
• Can purchase externally for $75/unit
• Uses component in final product sold for $200
• Other variable costs: $60 per final unit

Currently, divisions negotiate a transfer price of $65/unit.

requirements.question: Calculate Components Division profit on internal transfer at $65.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

(Transfer price - Variable cost) × Units = ($65 - $45) × 25,000 = $500,000 contribution

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the minimum transfer price for Components Division.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Minimum = Variable cost + Opportunity cost. Components has excess capacity (70,000 + 25,000 = 95,000 < 100,000), so no opportunity cost. Minimum = $45.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the maximum transfer price for Assembly Division.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Maximum = External purchase price = $75/unit. Assembly will not pay more internally than externally.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: If Components operates at full capacity with $80 external price, what is the minimum transfer price?
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

At full capacity: Minimum = Variable cost + Opportunity cost = $45 + ($80 - $45) = $80. Must forgo external sale to supply internally.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: What transfer price range allows both divisions to benefit (with excess capacity)?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Range = Minimum transfer price to Maximum transfer price = $45 to $75. Any price in this range benefits both divisions compared to their alternatives.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Minimum TP = Variable cost + Opportunity cost of selling internally
Maximum TP = Lower of external price or contribution margin
Excess capacity = Zero opportunity cost
Goal congruence: Internal transfer should benefit company overall

tbs.title: Business Combination - Acquisition Method
tbs.scenario:
On January 1, Year 1, Parent Corp. acquired 100% of Target Inc. for $5,000,000 cash.

Target's book values at acquisition:
| Account | Book Value | Fair Value |
|---------|------------|------------|
| Cash | $200,000 | $200,000 |
| Accounts Receivable | $400,000 | $380,000 |
| Inventory | $600,000 | $700,000 |
| Equipment (net) | $1,200,000 | $1,500,000 |
| Patents | $0 | $400,000 |
| Total Assets | $2,400,000 | $3,180,000 |
| Accounts Payable | $300,000 | $300,000 |
| Long-term Debt | $800,000 | $780,000 |
| Total Liabilities | $1,100,000 | $1,080,000 |

Remaining life of equipment: 10 years
Patent remaining life: 5 years

requirements.question: Calculate the fair value of net identifiable assets acquired.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

FV Assets - FV Liabilities = $3,180,000 - $1,080,000 = $2,100,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate goodwill from the acquisition.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Goodwill = Purchase price - FV of net identifiable assets = $5,000,000 - $2,100,000 = $2,900,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate Year 1 amortization of fair value adjustments.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Equipment: ($1,500,000 - $1,200,000) / 10 = $30,000. Patent: $400,000 / 5 = $80,000. Total = $110,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: What is the additional COGS recognized when inventory is sold?
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

FV inventory adjustment = $700,000 - $600,000 = $100,000. This flows through COGS when inventory is sold.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: How is goodwill treated post-acquisition?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Under ASC 350, goodwill is NOT amortized. It is tested for impairment at least annually at the reporting unit level.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Acquisition method: Record at fair value
Goodwill = Consideration - FV of net identifiable assets
Identified intangibles (patents) are separated from goodwill
FV adjustments amortize over remaining useful lives

tbs.title: Special Purpose Local Government Fund
tbs.scenario:
The City of Maplewood established a Capital Projects Fund for a new fire station:

Year 1 Transactions:
• Received bond proceeds: $10,000,000
• Received state grant: $2,000,000
• Recorded construction contract: $11,500,000
• Received progress billing from contractor: $4,000,000
• Paid contractor: $3,800,000
• Invested idle cash: $5,000,000
• Received investment income: $150,000
• Year-end encumbrances outstanding: $7,500,000

requirements.question: Calculate total revenues for the Capital Projects Fund.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Bond proceeds $10,000,000 + Grant $2,000,000 + Investment income $150,000 = $12,150,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate total expenditures for the year.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Expenditures = Progress billing received (work performed) = $4,000,000. Payment of $3,800,000 is not the expenditure—the work billed is.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate fund balance - restricted for construction.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Revenues $12,150,000 - Expenditures $4,000,000 = $8,150,000. This represents resources available for construction.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: How should encumbrances be reported?
options: As restricted or committed fund balance
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Outstanding encumbrances are NOT expenditures or liabilities. They represent committed resources and are reported as restricted/committed fund balance.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: How is the fire station reported on government-wide statements?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

On government-wide statements (accrual basis), construction costs are capitalized as Construction in Progress until the asset is complete and placed in service.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Capital projects funds use modified accrual basis
Bond proceeds are "other financing sources" (reported like revenue)
Encumbrances are budgetary, not GAAP liabilities
Government-wide uses accrual; fund uses modified accrual

tbs.title: Not-for-Profit Net Asset Classification
tbs.scenario:
Community Health Foundation (a not-for-profit) received the following during Year 1:

1. Unrestricted donations: $500,000
2. Multi-year pledge for operations (Year 1: $100K, Year 2: $100K): $200,000 total
3. Grant restricted for research equipment purchases: $400,000
4. Endowment gift (principal must be maintained permanently): $1,000,000
5. Donor-restricted for Year 2 program: $150,000
6. Investment return on endowment: $80,000 (unrestricted per donor)

Year 1 Activities:
• Purchased research equipment: $350,000
• Program expenses: $600,000
• Administrative expenses: $150,000

requirements.question: Calculate Year 1 net assets without donor restrictions (beginning = $0).
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Unrestricted gifts $500,000 + Y1 pledge $100,000 + Investment return $80,000 + Released from restriction $350,000 - Expenses ($600K + $150K) = $280,000. Wait: equipment purchase releases restriction. $500K + $100K + $80K + $350K (released) - $750K expenses = $280,000. Let me recalc: Inflows: $500K + $100K + $80K + $350K released = $1,030,000. Outflows: $750K expenses. Net = $280,000.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate net assets with donor restrictions at year-end.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Equipment grant: $400K - $350K used = $50K remaining. Y2 pledge: $100K. Year 2 program: $150K. Endowment: $1,000,000. Total = $1,300,000.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: How is the multi-year pledge recognized?
options: $200K in Year 1 with time restriction on Year 2 portion
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Multi-year pledges are recognized at present value when made. Year 2 portion is temporarily restricted (time restriction). Both portions recognized in Year 1.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: How is the equipment purchase reported?
options: Asset with release of $350,000 from restriction
Reduction in net assets with donor restrictions
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Equipment is capitalized as an asset. The $350,000 restriction is released (satisfaction of purpose restriction) and flows to net assets without donor restrictions.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Purpose restrictions: Released when purpose is met
Time restrictions: Released when time passes
Endowment principal = Permanently restricted
Investment return follows donor stipulation (or without restriction if silent)

tbs.title: Cost-Volume-Profit Analysis
tbs.scenario:
Precision Products sells a single product:

Selling price: $120 per unit
Variable costs: $72 per unit
Fixed costs: $240,000 per year
Current sales: 8,000 units
Tax rate: 25%

Management is evaluating strategies to improve profitability.

requirements.question: Calculate the contribution margin ratio.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

CM per unit = $120 - $72 = $48. CM ratio = $48 / $120 = 40%

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the break-even point in units.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

BE units = Fixed costs / CM per unit = $240,000 / $48 = 5,000 units

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate units needed for $120,000 after-tax profit.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Target pre-tax = $120,000 / (1 - 0.25) = $160,000. Units = (FC + Target) / CM = ($240,000 + $160,000) / $48 = 8,333 units

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the margin of safety in dollars at current sales.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Current sales = 8,000 × $120 = $960,000. BE sales = 5,000 × $120 = $600,000. MOS = $960,000 - $600,000 = $360,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate operating leverage at current volume.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

DOL = CM / Operating Income. CM = 8,000 × $48 = $384,000. OI = $384,000 - $240,000 = $144,000. DOL = $384,000 / $144,000 = 2.67

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: CM ratio = (Price - Variable cost) / Price
Target volume includes pre-tax profit requirement
MOS shows cushion before losses
High DOL = More sensitive to sales changes

tbs.title: Prospective Financial Information
tbs.scenario:
A client has prepared a 5-year financial projection for a bank loan:

**Assumptions:**
• Year 1 revenue: $10,000,000
• Revenue growth: 15% annually
• Gross margin: 35% (constant)
• Operating expenses: 20% of revenue (constant)
• Capital expenditures: $500,000 annually
• Working capital: 15% of revenue increase
• Debt: $3,000,000 at 8% interest
• Tax rate: 25%

**Projected Results:**
| Year | Revenue | EBIT | Net Income |
|------|---------|------|------------|
| 1 | $10.0M | $1.5M | $945K |
| 2 | $11.5M | $1.73M | $1.11M |
| 3 | $13.2M | $1.98M | $1.29M |
| 4 | $15.2M | $2.28M | $1.51M |
| 5 | $17.5M | $2.63M | $1.77M |

requirements.question: What type of prospective financial information is this?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

This appears to be a financial forecast based on expected conditions (specific growth rate, stable margins). A projection would use hypothetical "what-if" assumptions.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Verify Year 1 EBIT calculation.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Gross profit = $10M × 35% = $3.5M. Operating expenses = $10M × 20% = $2.0M. EBIT = $3.5M - $2.0M = $1.5M ✓

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Verify Year 1 Net Income calculation.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

EBIT $1.5M - Interest ($3M × 8% = $240K) = $1.26M EBT. Tax = $1.26M × 25% = $315K. Net Income = $1.26M - $315K = $945K ✓

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: What is the most significant assumption risk?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Sustained 15% annual growth for 5 years is aggressive and the highest-risk assumption. By Year 5, revenue would be 75% higher than Year 1, which may not be achievable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: What should accompany these projections for credibility?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Credible projections require: (1) historical financial support, (2) sensitivity/scenario analysis showing range of outcomes, and (3) clear disclosure of key assumptions and their basis.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Forecasts = Expected; Projections = Hypothetical
EBIT = Gross profit - Operating expenses
Sensitivity analysis tests assumption changes
Multi-year growth assumptions carry compounding risk

tbs.title: Capital Budgeting - NPV Analysis
tbs.scenario:
Horizon Corp. is evaluating a new production line investment:

Initial investment: $2,500,000
Project life: 5 years
Salvage value: $200,000
Annual cash operating savings: $750,000
Additional working capital required: $150,000 (recovered at end)

Tax rate: 25%
Cost of capital: 10%
Depreciation: Straight-line over 5 years

Present Value Factors at 10%:
Year 1: 0.909
Year 2: 0.826
Year 3: 0.751
Year 4: 0.683
Year 5: 0.621
PV of Annuity, 5 years: 3.791

requirements.question: Calculate annual depreciation expense.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Depreciable base = $2,500,000 - $200,000 = $2,300,000. Annual depreciation = $2,300,000 / 5 = $460,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate annual after-tax cash flows from operations.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Operating income = $750,000 - $460,000 depreciation = $290,000. Tax = $290,000 × 25% = $72,500. After-tax income = $217,500. Add back depreciation: $217,500 + $460,000 = $677,500

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate terminal cash flow (Year 5).
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Salvage $200,000 + Working capital recovery $150,000 = $350,000 terminal cash flow

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate Net Present Value.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

NPV = -Initial $2,500,000 - WC $150,000 + (Annual CF $677,500 × 3.791) + (Terminal $350,000 × 0.621) = -$2,650,000 + $2,568,533 + $217,350 = $135,883. Recalculate: Annual cash flow PV = $677,500 × 3.791 = $2,568,433.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: After-tax CF = (Revenue - Expenses)(1-T) + Depreciation × T
Working capital investment is recovered at project end
Salvage value affects both depreciation and terminal CF

tbs.title: Variance Analysis - Manufacturing
tbs.scenario:
Sterling Manufacturing produces widgets. Standard cost data:

Direct Materials:
• Standard: 3 lbs per unit @ $5 per lb
• Actual: 32,000 lbs purchased and used
• Actual price: $4.80 per lb
• Units produced: 10,000

Direct Labor:
• Standard: 2 hours per unit @ $20 per hour
• Actual: 21,500 hours worked
• Actual rate: $21 per hour

Variable Overhead:
• Standard: $8 per direct labor hour
• Actual: $180,000

Fixed Overhead:
• Budgeted: $200,000 (based on 10,000 units)
• Actual: $210,000

requirements.question: Calculate the direct materials price variance.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

MPV = (Standard price - Actual price) × Actual qty = ($5.00 - $4.80) × 32,000 = $6,400 Favorable

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the direct materials quantity variance.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

MQV = (Standard qty - Actual qty) × Standard price = (30,000 - 32,000) × $5 = -$10,000 Unfavorable

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the direct labor rate variance.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

LRV = (Standard rate - Actual rate) × Actual hours = ($20 - $21) × 21,500 = -$21,500 Unfavorable

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the direct labor efficiency variance.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

LEV = (Standard hours - Actual hours) × Standard rate = (20,000 - 21,500) × $20 = -$30,000 Unfavorable

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the variable overhead efficiency variance.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

VOH eff = (Standard hours - Actual hours) × Standard VOH rate = (20,000 - 21,500) × $8 = -$12,000 Unfavorable

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Price variance: Uses actual quantity
Quantity/Efficiency variance: Uses standard price/rate
Favorable = costs less than standard; Unfavorable = costs more

tbs.title: Cost-Volume-Profit Analysis
tbs.scenario:
Pacific Products sells two products with the following data:

Product A:
• Selling price: $100
• Variable cost: $60
• Sales mix: 60%

Product B:
• Selling price: $150
• Variable cost: $90
• Sales mix: 40%

Total fixed costs: $600,000
Tax rate: 30%
Target after-tax profit: $210,000

requirements.question: Calculate contribution margin per unit for Product A.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

CM(A) = Price $100 - Variable cost $60 = $40

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate weighted-average contribution margin.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

WACM = ($40 × 60%) + ($60 × 40%) = $24 + $24 = $48

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate break-even point in total units.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

BEP = Fixed costs / WACM = $600,000 / $48 = 12,500 units

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate units needed for target after-tax profit.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Pre-tax profit needed = $210,000 / (1 - 0.30) = $300,000. Units = ($600,000 + $300,000) / $48 = 18,750

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Use weighted-average CM for multi-product analysis
Convert after-tax profit to pre-tax for CVP
Mix percentages must sum to 100%

tbs.title: Transfer Pricing
tbs.scenario:
Division A of Consolidated Corp. produces Component X which can be sold externally or transferred to Division B.

Division A data:
• Variable production cost: $45 per unit
• Fixed production cost: $20 per unit (at capacity)
• External selling price: $80 per unit
• Capacity: 100,000 units
• External demand: 80,000 units

Division B data:
• Currently purchases externally at $75 per unit
• Needs: 30,000 units annually
• Final product selling price: $200 per unit

Company is decentralized with profit center structure.

requirements.question: Calculate minimum transfer price acceptable to Division A.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

With excess capacity (100K - 80K = 20K available), minimum = variable cost = $45. For units requiring displaced sales, minimum = $80.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate maximum transfer price acceptable to Division B.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Maximum = External purchase price = $75 (what Division B would otherwise pay)

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: If transfer price is set at $60, what happens to company profit?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Transfer saves $75 - $45 = $30 per unit on 30,000 units = $900,000. But 10,000 units displace external sales: lost CM = ($80-$45) × 10,000 = $350,000. Net benefit = $900,000 - $350,000 = $550,000 (approximately)

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: At what transfer price would Division A be indifferent between internal and external sales?
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

When displacing external sales, Division A is indifferent at external market price = $80

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Minimum TP = Variable cost + Opportunity cost
Maximum TP = External purchase price
Excess capacity changes opportunity cost calculation

tbs.title: Ratio Analysis - Comprehensive
tbs.scenario:
Beacon Industries - Selected Financial Data:

Balance Sheet:
• Cash: $150,000
• Accounts Receivable: $400,000
• Inventory: $500,000
• Total Current Assets: $1,050,000
• Total Assets: $3,000,000
