• Total Liabilities: $1,500,000
• Stockholders' Equity: $1,500,000

Income Statement:
• Net Sales: $4,000,000
• Cost of Goods Sold: $2,800,000
• Operating Income: $600,000
• Interest Expense: $100,000
• Net Income: $350,000

Shares Outstanding: 200,000
Market Price per Share: $35

requirements.question: Calculate the current ratio.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Current ratio = Current assets / Current liabilities = $1,050,000 / $600,000 = 1.75

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the debt-to-equity ratio.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

D/E = Total liabilities / Stockholders equity = $1,500,000 / $1,500,000 = 1.0

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate inventory turnover.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Inventory turnover = COGS / Inventory = $2,800,000 / $500,000 = 5.6 times

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate price-to-earnings ratio.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

EPS = Net income / Shares = $350,000 / 200,000 = $1.75. P/E = $35 / $1.75 = 20

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate times interest earned.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

TIE = Operating income / Interest expense = $600,000 / $100,000 = 6.0

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Liquidity ratios use current assets/liabilities
Activity ratios measure efficiency (turnover)
Coverage ratios assess debt-paying ability

tbs.title: Process Costing - FIFO Method
tbs.scenario:
Cascade Manufacturing uses FIFO process costing. Assembly Department data for October:

Beginning WIP:
• Units: 8,000 (Materials 100% complete, Conversion 60% complete)
• Prior month costs: Materials $40,000, Conversion $28,800

October activity:
• Units started: 42,000
• Units completed and transferred: 40,000
• Ending WIP: 10,000 (Materials 100%, Conversion 40%)

October costs added:
• Direct materials: $210,000
• Conversion costs: $175,000

requirements.question: Calculate equivalent units for materials (FIFO).
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Materials EUP = Complete BWIP 0% + Started & completed 32,000 (100%) + EWIP 10,000 (100%) = 0 + 32,000 + 10,000 = 42,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate equivalent units for conversion (FIFO).
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Conversion EUP = Complete BWIP 8,000 × 40% + Started & completed 32,000 + EWIP 10,000 × 40% = 3,200 + 32,000 + 4,000 = 39,200

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate cost per equivalent unit for materials.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Materials cost/EU = October materials $210,000 / Materials EU 42,000 = $5.00

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate cost per equivalent unit for conversion.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Conversion cost/EU = October conversion $175,000 / Conversion EU 39,200 = $4.47

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: FIFO: Current period costs / Current period equivalent units
Beginning WIP receives only completion work
Started & completed = Completed - Beginning WIP units

tbs.title: Economic Order Quantity
tbs.scenario:
Atlas Supply Company purchases a key component with the following data:

Annual demand: 36,000 units
Purchase price: $25 per unit
Ordering cost: $200 per order
Holding cost: 20% of unit cost per year
Lead time: 5 days
Safety stock: 300 units

Assume 360 working days per year.

requirements.question: Calculate the annual holding cost per unit.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Holding cost = 20% × $25 = $5 per unit per year

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the Economic Order Quantity.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

EOQ = √(2 × D × O / H) = √(2 × 36,000 × $200 / $5) = √2,880,000 = 1,697. Let me recalculate: √(2×36000×200/5) = √(14,400,000/5) = √2,880,000 = 1,697

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the reorder point.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Daily demand = 36,000 / 360 = 100 units. Lead time demand = 100 × 5 = 500. Reorder point = 500 + Safety stock 300 = 800 units

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate number of orders per year at EOQ.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Orders per year = Annual demand / EOQ = 36,000 / 2,400 = 15 orders (using rounded EOQ)

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: EOQ = √(2DO/H) where D=demand, O=ordering cost, H=holding cost
Reorder point = Lead time demand + Safety stock
Total cost minimized when ordering cost = holding cost

tbs.title: ROI and Residual Income
tbs.scenario:
Central Division of Apex Corp. has the following data:

Operating assets: $5,000,000
Operating income: $850,000
Cost of capital: 12%

The division is considering a new project:
• Required investment: $800,000
• Expected annual operating income: $120,000

Corporate uses ROI for performance evaluation.

requirements.question: Calculate current division ROI.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

ROI = Operating income / Operating assets = $850,000 / $5,000,000 = 17%

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate division ROI if project is accepted.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

New ROI = ($850,000 + $120,000) / ($5,000,000 + $800,000) = $970,000 / $5,800,000 = 16.72%

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate current residual income.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

RI = Operating income - (Operating assets × Cost of capital) = $850,000 - ($5,000,000 × 12%) = $850,000 - $600,000 = $250,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate project residual income.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Project RI = $120,000 - ($800,000 × 12%) = $120,000 - $96,000 = $24,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Will the division manager accept the project under ROI evaluation?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Project ROI = 15% ($120K/$800K) which is below current 17%. Under ROI evaluation, manager would reject even though project exceeds 12% cost of capital.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: ROI = Income / Investment (or Assets)
RI = Income - (Assets × Required rate)
ROI can cause managers to reject profitable projects

tbs.title: Activity-Based Costing
tbs.scenario:
Precision Products manufactures two products. ABC data:

Activity Cost Pools:
| Activity | Cost | Driver | Total Driver Qty |
|----------|------|--------|-----------------|
| Machine Setup | $180,000 | # of setups | 300 |
| Quality Inspection | $120,000 | # of inspections | 400 |
| Material Handling | $90,000 | # of moves | 600 |
| Machine Running | $210,000 | Machine hours | 7,000 |

Product data:
| | Product X | Product Y |
|---|-----------|-----------|
| Units produced | 10,000 | 5,000 |
| Setups | 100 | 200 |
| Inspections | 100 | 300 |
| Material moves | 200 | 400 |
| Machine hours | 2,000 | 5,000 |

requirements.question: Calculate the activity rate for Machine Setup.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Setup rate = $180,000 / 300 setups = $600 per setup

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate total overhead allocated to Product X.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

X overhead: Setups (100 × $600) + Inspections (100 × $300) + Moves (200 × $150) + Machine (2,000 × $30) = $60,000 + $30,000 + $30,000 + $60,000 = $180,000. Recheck rates: Inspection $300/insp, Moves $150/move, Machine $30/MH

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate overhead cost per unit for Product X.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Product X overhead per unit = $161,000 / 10,000 units = $16.10

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate overhead cost per unit for Product Y.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Y overhead: Setups (200 × $600) + Inspections (300 × $300) + Moves (400 × $150) + Machine (5,000 × $30) = $120,000 + $90,000 + $60,000 + $150,000 = $420,000 / 5,000 = $84 per unit

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Activity rate = Activity cost pool / Total driver quantity
Allocate cost = Activity rate × Product's driver usage
ABC often reveals low-volume products cost more

tbs.title: Internal Rate of Return

requirements.question: Calculate NPV at 10%.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

NPV@10% = -$500,000 + ($150,000 × 0.909) + ($180,000 × 0.826) + ($200,000 × 0.751) + ($150,000 × 0.683) = -$500,000 + $136,350 + $148,680 + $150,200 + $102,450 = $37,680

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate NPV at 15%.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

NPV@15% = -$500,000 + ($150,000 × 0.870) + ($180,000 × 0.756) + ($200,000 × 0.658) + ($150,000 × 0.572) = -$500,000 + $130,500 + $136,080 + $131,600 + $85,800 = -$16,020

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Based on the NPV calculations, the IRR is approximately:
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

NPV is positive at 10% and negative at 15%, so IRR is between 10% and 15% (closer to 14%).

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Should the project be accepted?
options: Yes - IRR exceeds cost of capital
No - IRR below cost of capital
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

IRR is approximately 14% which exceeds the 12% cost of capital. Project should be accepted.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: IRR is the rate where NPV = 0
If NPV positive at lower rate, negative at higher, IRR is between
Accept if IRR > Cost of capital

tbs.title: Flexible Budgeting
tbs.scenario:
Trident Manufacturing prepares flexible budgets. Budget formulas:

Variable costs per unit:
• Direct materials: $12
• Direct labor: $18
• Variable overhead: $6
• Variable selling: $4

Fixed costs (monthly):
• Manufacturing overhead: $150,000
• Selling and administrative: $80,000

Actual results for March:
• Units produced and sold: 12,000
• Direct materials: $150,000
• Direct labor: $225,000
• Variable overhead: $78,000
• Variable selling: $52,000
• Fixed manufacturing: $155,000
• Fixed S&A: $82,000

Original budget was based on 10,000 units.

requirements.question: Calculate flexible budget total variable costs for 12,000 units.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Variable costs = 12,000 × ($12 + $18 + $6 + $4) = 12,000 × $40 = $480,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate direct materials variance.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Flexible budget DM = 12,000 × $12 = $144,000. Actual = $150,000. Variance = $144,000 - $150,000 = -$6,000 Unfavorable

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate total flexible budget variance.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Flexible budget total = $480,000 + $230,000 = $710,000. Actual total = $150,000 + $225,000 + $78,000 + $52,000 + $155,000 + $82,000 = $742,000. Variance = -$32,000 Unfavorable

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate sales volume variance using contribution margin per unit of $40.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Volume variance = (Actual units - Budgeted units) × CM per unit = (12,000 - 10,000) × $40 = $80,000 Favorable

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Flexible budget adjusts for actual activity level
Flexible budget variance = Flexible budget - Actual
Sales volume variance = (Actual - Budget units) × CM

tbs.title: Weighted Average Cost of Capital
tbs.scenario:
Global Industries has the following capital structure:

Debt:
• Book value: $4,000,000
• Market value: $3,800,000
• Coupon rate: 6%
• Yield to maturity: 7%

Preferred Stock:
• Book value: $1,000,000
• Market value: $900,000
• Dividend rate: 8%
• Current yield: 9%

Common Equity:
• Book value: $5,000,000
• Market value: $8,000,000
• Required return (CAPM): 14%

Corporate tax rate: 25%

requirements.question: Calculate total market value of capital.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Total MV = Debt $3,800,000 + Preferred $900,000 + Common $8,000,000 = $12,700,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate after-tax cost of debt.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

After-tax cost of debt = YTM × (1 - Tax rate) = 7% × (1 - 0.25) = 5.25%

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate weight of common equity in capital structure.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Equity weight = $8,000,000 / $12,700,000 = 63%

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the weighted average cost of capital.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

WACC = (Debt weight × After-tax debt cost) + (Preferred weight × Preferred cost) + (Equity weight × Equity cost) = (0.30 × 5.25%) + (0.07 × 9%) + (0.63 × 14%) = 1.57% + 0.63% + 8.82% = 11.02%

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Use market values for weights (not book)
Only debt gets tax shield
Preferred stock cost = Dividend / Market price

tbs.title: Joint Product Costing
tbs.scenario:
Petrochem Industries processes crude oil into three products at a split-off point:

Joint costs before split-off: $900,000

Product data at split-off:
| Product | Units | Sales Value @ Split-off | Further Processing Cost | Final Sales Value |
|---------|-------|------------------------|------------------------|------------------|
| Gasoline | 100,000 gal | $400,000 | $80,000 | $520,000 |
| Diesel | 60,000 gal | $300,000 | $50,000 | $380,000 |
| Kerosene | 40,000 gal | $200,000 | $30,000 | $250,000 |

Total | | $900,000 | | |

requirements.question: Allocate joint costs to Gasoline using sales value at split-off method.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Gasoline allocation = ($400,000 / $900,000) × $900,000 = $400,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate net realizable value for Diesel.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

NRV = Final sales value - Further processing cost = $380,000 - $50,000 = $330,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Allocate joint costs to Kerosene using NRV method.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Total NRV = $440,000 + $330,000 + $220,000 = $990,000. Kerosene NRV = $220,000. Allocation = ($220,000 / $990,000) × $900,000 = $200,000. Recheck: Gasoline NRV = $520K-$80K = $440K.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Should Diesel be processed further?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Incremental revenue = $380,000 - $300,000 = $80,000. Incremental cost = $50,000. Net benefit = $30,000. Process further.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Joint costs are sunk - don't affect sell/process decision
NRV = Final sales value - Separable costs
Process further if incremental revenue > incremental cost

tbs.title: Balanced Scorecard Measures
tbs.scenario:
Gateway Services tracks the following Balanced Scorecard metrics:

Financial Perspective:
• Target ROI: 18%
• Target revenue growth: 12%

Customer Perspective:
• Target customer satisfaction: 90%
• Target customer retention: 85%

Internal Process:
• Target defect rate: 2%
• Target on-time delivery: 95%

Learning & Growth:
• Target employee turnover: 10%
• Target training hours per employee: 40

Actual results:
• ROI: 15%
• Revenue growth: 14%
• Customer satisfaction: 88%
• Customer retention: 87%
• Defect rate: 3%
• On-time delivery: 92%
• Employee turnover: 12%
• Training hours: 35

requirements.question: Which perspective shows the best performance vs. targets?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Customer: Satisfaction 88%/90% = 98%, Retention 87%/85% = 102%. Average ~100%. Customer perspective is closest to/exceeds targets.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: The defect rate variance in percentage points is:
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Defect rate: Target 2%, Actual 3%. Variance = 1 percentage point unfavorable (higher defects is bad).

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Which metrics are likely leading indicators of future financial performance?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Leading indicators predict future results: Customer satisfaction drives retention drives revenue. Training drives quality drives customer satisfaction.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Based on the results, which area needs the most improvement?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Learning & Growth: Turnover 12%/10% = 120% (bad), Training 35/40 = 87.5%. Both metrics underperforming significantly.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: BSC links four perspectives in cause-effect chain
Leading indicators predict; Lagging indicators confirm
Lower is better for: defects, turnover, returns

tbs.title: Make or Buy Decision
tbs.scenario:
Dynamo Corp. currently manufactures Part R7 for its products:

Annual requirements: 50,000 units

Current manufacturing costs:
• Direct materials: $8 per unit
• Direct labor: $12 per unit
• Variable overhead: $5 per unit
• Fixed overhead: $10 per unit (total $500,000)

An outside supplier offers to sell Part R7 for $28 per unit.

If outsourced:
• 40% of fixed overhead is avoidable
• Manufacturing space could be rented for $50,000 per year

requirements.question: Calculate variable cost to manufacture per unit.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Variable cost = DM $8 + DL $12 + VOH $5 = $25 per unit

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate relevant cost to manufacture (total).
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Total variable = 50,000 × $25 = $1,250,000. Avoidable fixed = $500,000 × 40% = $200,000. Total relevant = $1,250,000 + $200,000 (if we keep making, we incur this) = $1,450,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate relevant cost to buy (total).
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Purchase = 50,000 × $28 = $1,400,000. Less rental income = $1,400,000 - $50,000 = $1,350,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: What is the recommendation?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Buy cost $1,350,000 < Make cost $1,450,000. Annual savings = $100,000. Recommend buying.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Only include relevant (avoidable) costs
Allocated fixed costs are usually not relevant
Include opportunity costs (rental income)

tbs.title: Data Analytics - Regression Analysis
tbs.scenario:
A regression analysis was performed to predict monthly utility costs based on machine hours:

Regression output:
• Intercept (a): $15,000
• Coefficient for machine hours (b): $2.50
• R-squared: 0.89
• Standard error: $1,200
• Sample size: 24 months
• P-value for coefficient: 0.001

Next month's planned machine hours: 8,000

Confidence level for prediction: 95%

requirements.question: Calculate predicted utility cost for 8,000 machine hours.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Predicted cost = a + b × X = $15,000 + ($2.50 × 8,000) = $15,000 + $20,000 = $35,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: What does the R-squared value of 0.89 indicate?
options: 89% of cost variation is explained by machine hours
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

R-squared (coefficient of determination) indicates 89% of the variation in utility costs is explained by machine hours.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Is the machine hour coefficient statistically significant?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

P-value of 0.001 < 0.05 indicates the coefficient is statistically significant at 95% confidence level.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: What is the approximate 95% confidence interval for the prediction?
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Approximate CI = ±2 × Standard error = ±2 × $1,200 = ±$2,400. Range: $32,600 to $37,400.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Y = a + bX is the regression equation
R-squared measures explanatory power (0 to 1)
P-value < 0.05 = significant at 95% confidence

tbs.title: Special Order Decision
tbs.scenario:
Quantum Products operates at 70% capacity. Regular operations:

• Normal capacity: 100,000 units
• Current production: 70,000 units
• Selling price: $50 per unit
• Variable manufacturing cost: $28 per unit
• Variable selling cost: $6 per unit
• Fixed manufacturing cost: $800,000
• Fixed selling cost: $200,000

Special order received:
• Quantity: 20,000 units
• Price offered: $35 per unit
• No variable selling costs (direct shipment)
• Special packaging: $1.50 per unit additional

requirements.question: Calculate relevant variable cost per unit for special order.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Relevant variable = Manufacturing $28 + Special packaging $1.50 = $29.50 (no selling cost)

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate contribution margin per unit on special order.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

CM = Price $35 - Relevant variable $29.50 = $5.50 per unit

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate total incremental profit from special order.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Incremental profit = 20,000 units × $5.50 = $110,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Should Quantum accept the special order?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Accept because: (1) Excess capacity exists, (2) Positive contribution margin, (3) No impact on regular sales.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Special orders use incremental analysis
Fixed costs are usually irrelevant if capacity exists
Accept if price > incremental cost

tbs.title: DuPont Analysis
tbs.scenario:
Compare two divisions of Consolidated Industries:

Division A:
• Sales: $10,000,000
• Net income: $600,000
• Total assets: $5,000,000
• Total equity: $3,000,000

Division B:
• Sales: $8,000,000
• Net income: $640,000
• Total assets: $4,000,000
• Total equity: $2,000,000

Corporate target ROE: 18%

requirements.question: Calculate Division A's profit margin.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Profit margin = Net income / Sales = $600,000 / $10,000,000 = 6%

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate Division B's asset turnover.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Asset turnover = Sales / Total assets = $8,000,000 / $4,000,000 = 2.0

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate Division A's ROE using DuPont formula.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

ROE = Margin × Turnover × Equity Multiplier = 6% × 2.0 × 1.67 = 20%

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate Division B's ROE.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

ROE = ROA × Equity multiplier = (8% × 2.0) × 2.0 = 32%. Or directly: $640,000 / $2,000,000 = 32%

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: DuPont: ROE = Profit margin × Asset turnover × Equity multiplier
Equity multiplier = Assets / Equity = 1 + (Debt/Equity)
Higher leverage increases ROE (and risk)

tbs.title: Absorption vs Variable Costing
tbs.scenario:
Nexus Manufacturing data for Year 1:

Production and sales:
• Units produced: 50,000
• Units sold: 45,000
• Beginning inventory: 0

Costs:
• Direct materials: $10 per unit
• Direct labor: $15 per unit
• Variable manufacturing overhead: $5 per unit
• Fixed manufacturing overhead: $300,000
• Variable selling: $3 per unit sold
• Fixed selling and administrative: $150,000

Selling price: $60 per unit

requirements.question: Calculate product cost per unit under absorption costing.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Absorption: DM $10 + DL $15 + VOH $5 + FOH ($300,000/50,000) $6 = $36 per unit

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate product cost per unit under variable costing.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Variable: DM $10 + DL $15 + VOH $5 = $30 per unit (excludes fixed MOH)

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate operating income under absorption costing.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Sales = 45,000 × $60 = $2,700,000. COGS = 45,000 × $36 = $1,620,000. Gross margin = $1,080,000. Less: Variable selling (45,000 × $3) = $135,000. Fixed S&A = $150,000. Operating income = $795,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the difference between absorption and variable income.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Difference = Change in inventory × Fixed OH rate = (50,000 - 45,000) × $6 = 5,000 × $6 = $30,000. Absorption income higher when inventory increases.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Absorption: Fixed MOH in product cost
Variable: Fixed MOH is period cost
Difference = Change in inventory × Fixed MOH rate

tbs.title: Payback and Discounted Payback
tbs.scenario:
Stellar Investments evaluates a project:

Initial investment: $450,000
Annual cash flows (after-tax):
• Year 1: $100,000
• Year 2: $150,000
• Year 3: $175,000
• Year 4: $200,000
• Year 5: $125,000

Cost of capital: 10%

PV factors at 10%:
Year 1: 0.909, Year 2: 0.826, Year 3: 0.751, Year 4: 0.683, Year 5: 0.621

requirements.question: Calculate simple payback period in years.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Cumulative: Y1 $100K, Y2 $250K, Y3 $425K, Y4 $625K. After Y3: $450K - $425K = $25K remaining. Partial Y4: $25K / $200K = 0.125 years. Payback = 3.125 years

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate discounted cash flow for Year 3.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Y3 DCF = $175,000 × 0.751 = $131,425

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate discounted payback period in years.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

PV: Y1 $90,900, Y2 $123,900, Y3 $131,425, Y4 $136,600, Y5 $77,625. Cumulative: Y1 $91K, Y2 $215K, Y3 $346K, Y4 $483K. After Y3: $450K - $346K = $104K needed. Y4 recovery: $104K / $136.6K = 0.76 years. DPB = 3.76 years

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Which method better accounts for time value of money?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Discounted payback considers time value by using present values. Simple payback ignores TVM. NPV is superior to both for decision-making.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Simple payback ignores time value of money
Discounted payback = time to recover PV of investment
Both ignore cash flows after payback period

tbs.title: Pro Forma Financial Statement Review
tbs.scenario:
TechMerge Corp. is preparing pro forma financial statements for a proposed acquisition. The transaction details:

Acquiring Company (TechMerge):
• Revenue: $50 million
• Net income: $5 million
• Total assets: $40 million

Target Company (InnoSoft):
• Revenue: $20 million
• Net income: $2 million (includes one-time gain of $500,000)
• Total assets: $15 million

Pro Forma Adjustments Proposed:
1. Eliminate InnoSoft's one-time gain
2. Add expected synergies of $3 million annual cost savings
3. Record goodwill of $8 million from the acquisition
4. Amortize customer relationships intangible over 5 years ($2M, so $400K/year)
5. Record acquisition financing interest expense ($500K annually)

requirements.question: Calculate pro forma revenue.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Pro forma revenue = TechMerge ($50M) + InnoSoft ($20M) = $70M. Revenue is simply combined.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate pro forma net income with only the required adjustments (eliminate one-time, add intangible amortization, add interest expense).
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Combined net income ($7M) - One-time gain ($500K) - Amortization ($400K) - Interest ($500K) = $5.6M. Note: Synergies should NOT be included per SEC rules.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Why should synergies NOT be included in pro forma statements per SEC rules?
options: Synergies are too small to matter
Synergies are speculative and not factually supportable
Synergies only apply to tax calculations
The SEC requires separate synergy disclosure
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

SEC regulations (Regulation S-X Article 11) require pro forma adjustments to be factually supportable. Anticipated synergies are speculative and cannot be included.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Which type of prospective financial statement represents management's best estimate based on expected conditions?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

A financial forecast presents management's best estimate of expected financial results. Projections present "what-if" scenarios that may not be expected to occur.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Pro forma shows as-if the transaction occurred
Adjustments must be factually supportable
Synergies are too speculative for pro forma
Distinguish forecast (expected) vs projection (hypothetical)

tbs.title: Comprehensive Variance Analysis
tbs.scenario:
Precision Products Inc. manufactures a single product using standard costing. The following standards were established:

Standard Cost Card (per unit):
• Direct Materials: 4 pounds @ $12/lb = $48
• Direct Labor: 2.5 hours @ $20/hr = $50
• Variable Overhead: 2.5 DLH @ $8/DLH = $20
• Fixed Overhead: 2.5 DLH @ $6/DLH = $15

Budgeted production: 10,000 units
Actual production: 9,500 units

Actual Results for the Period:
• Direct materials purchased: 40,000 pounds @ $11.80/lb
• Direct materials used: 38,500 pounds
• Direct labor: 24,200 hours @ $20.50/hr
• Actual variable overhead: $188,000
• Actual fixed overhead: $152,000

requirements.question: Calculate the direct materials price variance.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Material Price Variance = (Actual Price - Standard Price) × Actual Qty Purchased = ($11.80 - $12.00) × 40,000 = -$8,000 (Favorable)

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the direct materials efficiency variance.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Standard Qty Allowed = 9,500 × 4 = 38,000 lbs. MEV = (Actual Qty Used - Std Qty Allowed) × Std Price = (38,500 - 38,000) × $12 = $6,000 Unfavorable

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the direct labor rate variance.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Labor Rate Variance = (Actual Rate - Standard Rate) × Actual Hours = ($20.50 - $20.00) × 24,200 = $12,100 Unfavorable

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the direct labor efficiency variance.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Standard Hours Allowed = 9,500 × 2.5 = 23,750 hrs. LEV = (Actual Hours - Std Hours) × Std Rate = (24,200 - 23,750) × $20 = $9,000 Unfavorable

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the fixed overhead volume variance.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Volume Variance = (Budgeted - Standard Hours Allowed) × Fixed OH Rate = (25,000 - 23,750) × $6 = $7,500 Unfavorable (production lower than budget)

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Price variance uses quantity purchased; efficiency variance uses quantity used
Standard quantity allowed = Actual production × Standard per unit
Fixed OH volume variance measures production efficiency vs. budget
Favorable = actual less than standard (savings)
